items.title: USDA posts updated list of SNAP food-restriction waivers with new implementation dates
items.summary: USDA FNS updated its SNAP food-restriction waiver list with multiple state waivers approved, including Florida (target implementation 04/20/26) restricting soda, energy drinks, candy and prepared desserts, and Colorado (04/30/26) restricting soft drinks. The list also includes Arkansas (07/01/26) and Texas (04/01/26) among others, indicating rollout dates extending into 2028 depending on the state.

items.title: CMS issues bulletin on Medicaid/CHIP managed care monitoring, including encounter data and SDP reporting
items.summary: CMS released a March 12, 2026 CMCS Informational Bulletin outlining Medicaid/CHIP managed care oversight expectations, including monitoring prior-authorization denial trends and enforcing quantitative network adequacy standards for OB/GYN care. The bulletin also reminds states to use audited financial and encounter data and notes that T-MSIS fields for state directed payment (SDP) data are due no later than September 2026.

items.title: HUD rescinds multiple COVID-era PIH notices affecting HCV (Section 8) and public housing guidance
items.summary: HUD Notice PIH 2026-06 (issued March 16, 2026) rescinds a list of COVID-19-related PIH notices and partially rescinds prior guidance affecting Housing Choice Voucher (HCV) and public housing programs. The notice also amends PIH 2020-32 by striking certain remote-hearing language-access instructions while stating PHAs must still comply with federal language access laws.
items.source: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)

items.title: Federal Register notice opens comment window on LIHEAP quarterly reports information collection
items.summary: The Federal Register published a March 4, 2026 notice for OMB review of LIHEAP Quarterly Reports (OMB Control No. 0970-0589) as ACF seeks a 3-year extension and proposes changes intended to reduce burden. Comments are due by April 3, 2026.

items.title: IRS: 2026 filing season opened Jan. 26; early EITC/ACTC refunds expected by March 2
items.summary: The IRS opened the 2026 filing season on January 26, 2026 and said most EITC and Additional Child Tax Credit (ACTC) refunds will be available by March 2, 2026 for taxpayers using direct deposit with no issues. The IRS said Where’s My Refund? would show projected deposit dates for most early EITC/ACTC filers by February 21, 2026.

items.title: IRS reminds taxpayers IRS Free File available for 2025 returns with AGI $89,000 or less
items.summary: In a January 29, 2026 tax tip, the IRS highlighted that IRS Free File guided software is available for eligible taxpayers with 2025 adjusted gross income (AGI) of $89,000 or less. The IRS also reiterated that most e-file refunds are issued within 21 days when returns are complete and accurate and direct deposit is selected.

items.title: AP: New Medicaid work requirements require 80 hours/month and major state system upgrades ahead of Jan. 1 start
items.summary: The Associated Press reports that new Medicaid eligibility mandates will require certain adults ages 19–64 (without young children) to work or do community service at least 80 hours per month (or be half-time students), with eligibility checks moving to every six months. AP cites CBO estimates that the provisions save $388 billion over a decade with about 6 million fewer insured, and reports a $200 million federal allotment to states even as projected state implementation costs exceed $1 billion.

items.title: Reuters: Judge blocks USDA from cutting SNAP admin funds over states’ refusal to provide recipient data
items.summary: Reuters reports a federal judge issued an injunction preventing USDA from cutting off administrative funding for 22 states unless they turn over SNAP recipient data such as names, addresses and immigration status. The decision notes about 42 million people receive SNAP and raises concerns about data sharing protocols.

items.title: NPR: SNAP work requirement expansion begins rolling out; some states had waivers delaying enforcement past March 1
items.summary: NPR reports expanded SNAP work requirements tied to President Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act began rolling out across states and counties around March 1, 2026, with some states enforcing earlier and others receiving waivers to delay. NPR says expanded groups subject to the 80-hours-per-month requirement include veterans, people experiencing homelessness, former foster youth, parents with children older than 14, and some adults ages 55–64.

items.title: Reuters: States urge Congress to delay SNAP cost shifts (up to 75% admin share in FY2027; up to 15% benefit share in FY2028)
items.summary: Reuters reports state and local government groups asked Congress to delay SNAP cost-shift provisions in President Trump’s tax and spending law. The provisions would increase states’ share of SNAP administrative costs to as much as 75% starting in fiscal year 2027 and require states to pay up to 15% of SNAP benefits starting in fiscal year 2028 (with the percentage tied to payment error rates), compared with prior full federal funding of benefits.

items.title: USDA sets FY2026 WIC fruit-and-vegetable cash-value benefit amounts and notes April 2026 compliance deadline
items.summary: USDA FNS guidance lists FY2026 monthly WIC cash-value benefit amounts of $26 for children, $48 for pregnant/postpartum participants, and $52 for fully or partially breastfeeding participants, reflecting an 11.8% inflation adjustment over the FY2022 baseline. The memo also notes WIC state agencies must authorize at least one non-fresh form of fruits and vegetables (frozen, canned, and/or dried) by April 2026.

items.title: SSA posts 2026 work incentive thresholds (SSI FBR, SGA, trial work period, student exclusion)
items.summary: SSA’s 2026 Red Book update lists the SSI Federal Benefit Rate as $994/month for an eligible individual and $1,491/month for an eligible couple. It also lists 2026 Substantial Gainful Activity at $1,690/month (non-blind) and $2,830/month (blind), a Trial Work Period threshold of $1,210/month, and a Student Earned-Income Exclusion of up to $9,730/year ($2,410/month until the yearly max is reached).
